pages.name: sqli_1
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 1: Fingerprint the Target

elements.name: sqli_context
elements.html: The internal web portal at 192.168.1.55 has a login form and an account lookup page. You suspect the inputs might not be handled safely on the backend. You need to confirm whether there's an injection point before committing time to this path.

elements.name: sqli_fingerprint
elements.title: How do you probe the target for SQL injection?

choices.text: Manually send a single quote into a parameter and observe how the server responds [costs 1 min]
Run an automated detection scan against the login form [costs 2 min]
Intercept requests in a proxy and review each parameter by hand [costs 3 min]
View the page source to look for hidden fields or clues about how queries are built [costs 1 min]

pages.name: sqli_1_source_branch
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 1.1: Source Review

elements.name: sqli_source_html
elements.html: The page source reveals a hidden field: <input type="hidden" name="redirect" value="/dashboard">. The login form posts to /auth/login with fields username, password, and redirect. No obvious client-side sanitization. The redirect parameter looks interesting — it may be passed directly to a query.

elements.name: sqli_source_choice
elements.title: You found a hidden redirect parameter. What do you do?

choices.text: Tamper with the redirect field — inject a quote and see if it causes an error [costs 1 min]
Ignore the redirect field and focus on the username/password fields instead [costs 1 min]
Feed the full request to an automated scanner targeting all three parameters [costs 2 min]

pages.name: sqli_2_redirect_branch
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 2 (Redirect Field): Probe the Parameter

elements.name: sqli_redirect_html
elements.html: You inject a single quote into the redirect field. The server throws a 500 error with no useful output — just a generic internal server error page. The redirect field may be injectable, but it produces no visible feedback. This is going to be slow.

elements.name: sqli_redirect_choice
elements.title: The redirect field errors silently. What do you do?

choices.text: Attempt time-based blind injection on the redirect field [costs 4 min]
Drop the redirect field and pivot to testing the username field instead [costs 1 min]

pages.name: sqli_2_blind_redirect_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Redirect Parameter

elements.name: sqli_redirect_dead_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

After several minutes of blind injection attempts on the redirect parameter, you confirm it is injectable — but the backend query it feeds into only writes to an access log table. There is no path from here to user credentials or the transfer system. You've burned time on a dead end.

elements.name: sqli_redirect_dead_choice
elements.title: The redirect parameter leads nowhere useful. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and try the username field instead [costs 2 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_2
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 2: Confirm the Vulnerability
pages.visibleIf: {chosen_path} = 'sqli' and ({sqli_fingerprint} = 'manual_quote' or {sqli_fingerprint} = 'sqlmap_detect' or {sqli_fingerprint} = 'burp_intercept' or {sqli_source_choice} = 'test_username' or {sqli_source_choice} = 'run_sqlmap_now' or {sqli_redirect_choice} = 'pivot_to_username' or {sqli_redirect_dead_choice} = 'retry_sqli') and {timedone} <> true

elements.name: sqli_error_html
elements.html: The server is returning verbose error output when you tamper with input — database internals are leaking into the response. You can see fragments like You have an error in your SQL syntax and a partial query structure. Injection is confirmed on the username field.

elements.name: sqli_exploit
elements.title: Injection is confirmed. What is your exploitation approach?

choices.text: Inject a classic bypass payload into the login form to authenticate without valid credentials [costs 1 min]
Use a UNION-based payload to pull data from tables directly into the page response [costs 3 min]
Run a full automated database dump [costs 4 min]
Use time-based blind injection to infer data without visible output [costs 5 min]

pages.name: sqli_3_blind_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Blind Injection

elements.name: sqli_blind_dead_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

You begin issuing time-delay payloads. The server responds consistently at first, but the WAF begins throttling after several rounds — your delay measurements are no longer reliable. Extracting a full user table this way would take far longer than your remaining window. The approach is too slow to continue.

elements.name: sqli_blind_dead_choice
elements.title: Blind injection is throttled and too slow. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and try a different injection technique [costs 2 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_3
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 3: Choose Your Target Account
pages.visibleIf: {chosen_path} = 'sqli' and ({sqli_exploit} = 'auth_bypass' or {sqli_exploit} = 'union_select' or {sqli_exploit} = 'sqlmap_full' or {sqli_blind_dead_choice} = 'retry_sqli') and {timedone} <> true

elements.name: sqli_dump_html
elements.html:
You've pulled data from the users table. It contains usernames, hashed passwords, and role fields. You spot several accounts:
  • admin — role: admin
  • jsmith — role: employee
  • svc_transfer_xk9 — role: transfer_approver
  • root — role: superuser
  • audit_log_svc — role: read_only
One of these might be the key. Choose wrong and you'll waste time — or trigger monitoring.

elements.name: sqli_account
elements.title: Which account do you target?

choices.text: admin — sounds like the most powerful account [costs 2 min]
jsmith — a real employee, low-profile, might have portal access [costs 2 min]
svc_transfer_xk9 — unusual name, but 'transfer_approver' is exactly what you need [costs 1 min]
root — if you can get in here, you own everything [costs 5 min]
audit_log_svc — read-only access, but maybe you can escalate [costs 2 min]

pages.name: sqli_3_honeypot
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Honeypot Accounts

elements.name: sqli_honeypot_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END — HONEYPOT TRIGGERED

These accounts are monitored traps. Accessing admin or root immediately flags your session. The portal logs your source IP and sends an alert to the security team. Neither account has transfer approval rights — they exist purely to catch attackers. You've burned time and exposed your presence on the network.

elements.name: sqli_honeypot_choice
elements.title: You tripped a honeypot. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and target a different account [costs 3 min penalty]
Cut your losses and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_3_jsmith_branch
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 3.1: Employee Account

elements.name: sqli_jsmith_html
elements.html: You authenticate as jsmith. The portal loads — employee dashboard with internal messaging, a document viewer, and a Finance Operations link that appears greyed out. jsmith doesn't have transfer approval rights, but there may be something useful here.

elements.name: sqli_jsmith_choice
elements.title: You're in as jsmith. What do you do?

choices.text: Read jsmith's internal messages — there may be credentials or routing info [costs 1 min]
Try to access the greyed-out Finance Operations link [costs 1 min]
Poke around the portal for misconfigured access controls [costs 2 min]
jsmith has no transfer rights — go back and target a different account [costs 2 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_3_jsmith_messages
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 3.2: jsmith's Inbox

elements.name: sqli_jsmith_messages_html
elements.html:
jsmith's inbox has three messages:

📧 IT Support: 'Your temporary password has been reset to Temp#2024! — please change it after login.'
📧 Finance Ops: 'Reminder: all transfers above $10k must be approved through the svc_transfer_xk9 service account dashboard.'
📧 HR: 'Your annual review is scheduled for Friday.'

The Finance Ops message confirms the service account's purpose. The temporary password might have been reused somewhere.

elements.name: sqli_jsmith_messages_choice
elements.title: Useful intel in the inbox. What do you do with it?

choices.text: Try the temporary password against svc_transfer_xk9 directly [costs 1 min]
Use the confirmed account name and target svc_transfer_xk9 through injection [costs 1 min]
Keep digging through jsmith's portal for more leads [costs 2 min]

pages.name: sqli_3_temp_password_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Password Reuse

elements.name: sqli_temp_password_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

You attempt to log in as svc_transfer_xk9 with Temp#2024!. The portal rejects it — service accounts don't use the employee password policy and this password was never set on this account. After a few more guesses, the portal locks the service account for 5 minutes and logs the failed attempts. You've burned time and flagged the account.

elements.name: sqli_temp_password_choice
elements.title: The account is now locked out. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and approach svc_transfer_xk9 through SQL injection instead [costs 3 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_3_jsmith_finance_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Finance Operations Link

elements.name: sqli_finance_dead_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

Clicking the Finance Operations link redirects to /finance/ops?user=jsmith&role=employee and returns a 403 Forbidden. You try tampering with the role and user parameters in the URL — the server validates both against the database on every request and ignores whatever you pass in the URL. Client-side parameter tampering is not possible here. jsmith simply does not have access.

elements.name: sqli_finance_dead_choice
elements.title: URL tampering is blocked server-side. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and target a different account through SQL injection [costs 2 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_3_jsmith_privesc_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Privilege Escalation

elements.name: sqli_privesc_dead_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

You spend time probing jsmith's portal session for misconfigured endpoints, IDOR opportunities, and role-check bypasses. The application enforces access control server-side consistently — every restricted page re-validates the session role against the database. There is no client-side bypass available from jsmith's session. This account is a dead end for privilege escalation.

elements.name: sqli_privesc_dead_choice
elements.title: No privilege escalation path from jsmith. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and target a different account through SQL injection [costs 2 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_3_jsmith_more_intel_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Further Recon

elements.name: sqli_more_intel_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

You spend additional time browsing jsmith's portal — documents, settings, and shared files. Nothing useful surfaces. The only actionable intel was already in the inbox. You've wasted time digging through HR documents and internal memos.

elements.name: sqli_more_intel_choice
elements.title: Nothing else useful here. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and approach svc_transfer_xk9 through SQL injection [costs 2 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_3_audit_branch
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 3.3: Audit Log Account

elements.name: sqli_audit_html
elements.html: You authenticate as audit_log_svc. The account has read-only access — you can browse transaction logs, user activity records, and system events, but cannot initiate or approve any actions. The logs are detailed though. You can see recent activity from every account, including svc_transfer_xk9.

elements.name: sqli_audit_choice
elements.title: You have read-only audit access. What do you look for?

choices.text: Read svc_transfer_xk9's recent activity — look for session patterns or tokens [costs 1 min]
Pull all recent transfer records to understand the approval workflow [costs 2 min]
Audit access is useless for your goal — go back and target a better account [costs 2 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_3_audit_svc_logs_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Audit Log Analysis

elements.name: sqli_audit_svc_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

The activity logs show svc_transfer_xk9 last authenticated 4 minutes ago. The log entries contain a partial session token fragment — but it's only the payload section of a signed JWT. Without the server's signing key, you cannot forge a valid token. The transfer records reveal the workflow but give you no credentials or exploitable access. Read-only is read-only.

elements.name: sqli_audit_dead_choice
elements.title: Audit access leads nowhere actionable. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and target svc_transfer_xk9 directly through SQL injection [costs 2 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_4
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Step 4: Access the Service Account
pages.visibleIf: {chosen_path} = 'sqli' and ({sqli_account} = 'svc_transfer_xk9' or {sqli_honeypot_choice} = 'retry_sqli' or {sqli_jsmith_messages_choice} = 'go_back_svc' or {sqli_jsmith_choice} = 'abandon_jsmith' or {sqli_finance_dead_choice} = 'retry_sqli' or {sqli_privesc_dead_choice} = 'retry_sqli' or {sqli_more_intel_choice} = 'retry_sqli' or {sqli_audit_choice} = 'abandon_audit' or {sqli_audit_dead_choice} = 'retry_sqli' or {sqli_temp_password_choice} = 'retry_sqli' or {sqli_blind_dead_choice} = 'retry_sqli') and {timedone} <> true

elements.name: sqli_svc_access_html
elements.html: You target svc_transfer_xk9 directly through the injection point. The account's password hash is in the users table — a bcrypt hash with cost factor 12. You could try cracking it offline, bypass authentication entirely, manipulate the session table, or reset the password through injection.

elements.name: sqli_svc_method
elements.title: How do you attempt to gain access to svc_transfer_xk9?

choices.text: Inject an auth bypass payload directly into the login query [costs 1 min]
Extract the bcrypt hash and attempt to crack it offline [costs 5 min]
Inject a crafted session row into the sessions table to create an active session without logging in [costs 3 min]
Use an UPDATE injection to overwrite the account's password hash [costs 2 min]

pages.name: sqli_4_crack_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Hash Cracking

elements.name: sqli_crack_dead_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

The bcrypt hash has a cost factor of 12. At your available compute rate, cracking it would take several hours at minimum — far beyond your remaining time window. Service account passwords are typically long, randomly generated strings that won't appear in any wordlist. This approach is not viable.

elements.name: sqli_crack_dead_choice
elements.title: Cracking is not viable in your time window. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and try a different method to access the account [costs 2 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_4_session_inject_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Session Table Injection

elements.name: sqli_session_inject_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

You craft an INSERT statement targeting the sessions table with a UUID you generated and a future expiry timestamp. The injection executes — but when you load the portal with your crafted cookie, the server returns a 401. The application does not validate sessions from the database alone; it also checks a secondary in-memory token store that you have no access to. Your injected session row is ignored.

elements.name: sqli_session_inject_choice
elements.title: The session injection approach is blocked by in-memory validation. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and try a different method to access the account [costs 2 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_4_reset_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Password Reset via Injection

elements.name: sqli_reset_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

You craft an UPDATE injection to overwrite the password hash for svc_transfer_xk9 with a hash you generated. The injection appears to execute with no error — but when you try to log in with your new password, the portal rejects it. The application uses a secondary authentication factor for service accounts: a hardware-bound TOTP token tied to the account's registered device. A password alone is not sufficient. You cannot produce the second factor.

elements.name: sqli_reset_choice
elements.title: The service account requires a second factor you can't produce. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and try a different method to access the account [costs 2 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection and switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_4_auth_bypass_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Dead End: Auth Bypass

elements.name: sqli_auth_bypass_html
elements.html:
// DEAD END

Your auth bypass payload authenticates you into a portal session as svc_transfer_xk9 — but the transfer approval dashboard immediately prompts for a hardware TOTP code before displaying any transfer controls. The second factor is tied to a physical device registered to the service account. Without that device, the dashboard is inaccessible. The session is valid but useless. SQL injection got you through the front door — the vault has a second lock you cannot pick from here.

elements.name: sqli_auth_bypass_choice
elements.title: You're in the session but locked out of the transfer dashboard by MFA. What do you do?

choices.text: Go back and look for another angle within SQL Injection [costs 3 min penalty]
Abandon SQL Injection — this path cannot complete the transfer [costs 5 min penalty]

pages.name: sqli_final_dead
pages.title: Path A — SQL Injection | Full Dead End
pages.visibleIf: {chosen_path} = 'sqli' and ({sqli_crack_dead_choice} = 'retry_sqli' or {sqli_session_inject_choice} = 'retry_sqli' or {sqli_reset_choice} = 'retry_sqli' or {sqli_auth_bypass_choice} = 'retry_sqli') and {timedone} <> true

elements.name: sqli_final_dead_html
elements.html:
// FULL DEAD END — PATH A EXHAUSTED

You have now attempted every available method to leverage SQL Injection against this target:
  • Hash cracking — blocked by bcrypt cost factor and time constraints
  • Session injection — blocked by in-memory secondary token validation
  • Password reset — blocked by hardware TOTP second factor
  • Auth bypass — blocked by hardware TOTP second factor on the transfer dashboard
The SQL Injection path cannot complete the transfer. The target application's MFA design specifically prevents this attack chain from reaching the transfer controls. You must switch paths.

elements.name: sqli_final_dead_choice
elements.title: SQL Injection cannot complete the objective. What do you do?

choices.text: Switch to a different attack path [costs 5 min penalty]
